question_text: A referral lists wound care, a new insulin schedule, and transportation concerns. Which RN case management action best validates the intake source before planning?
answer_a: Compare the referral with patient report, caregiver input, medication information, and available records.
answer_b: Accept the referral as complete because it came from a discharge source.
answer_c: Begin payer communication before reviewing clinical or functional risks.
answer_d: Document only the diagnosis and defer all validation until the next visit.
rationale: Validation requires comparing referral data with the patient, caregiver, medication, and record sources so the care plan is based on reliable and current intake findings.

question_text: Which interview approach best supports patient-centered intake during M02?
answer_a: Ask only closed yes/no questions so the intake can be finished quickly.
answer_b: Clarify what changed, what the patient understands, what the patient wants, and what barriers may affect follow-through.
answer_c: Let the caregiver answer all questions to reduce patient burden.
answer_d: Start with certificate and course completion rules before discussing patient care.
rationale: Patient-centered intake combines current status, patient goals, understanding, and barriers; this creates a practical baseline for case management planning.

question_text: During intake, the patient reports using the old insulin dose because the discharge instructions are confusing. What is the best RN case management response?
answer_a: Tell the patient which insulin dose to use until the provider visit.
answer_b: Ignore the issue because medication questions are outside case management.
answer_c: Document the discrepancy, assess understanding, teach safe follow-up within scope, and coordinate prompt clarification.
answer_d: Mark the referral complete because the medication list already exists.
rationale: The RN case manager should not change orders, but should recognize medication risk, document objective findings, educate within scope, and coordinate clarification.

question_text: A caregiver reports the patient almost fell during a bathroom transfer and the ordered walker has not arrived. Which priority is most appropriate?
answer_a: Treat the walker as an equipment preference and address it after routine paperwork.
answer_b: Focus only on the wound order because falls are not part of intake.
answer_c: Wait for the patient to fall before documenting functional risk.
answer_d: Escalate and coordinate fall-risk follow-up, equipment status, and caregiver support because immediate safety may be affected.
rationale: Near falls and missing mobility equipment are functional safety risks that can change the priority order and require timely coordination or escalation.

question_text: A patient understands the care plan but cannot reach follow-up appointments and reports limited food access. How should the RN case manager treat these findings?
answer_a: Include them as care plan barriers because they may affect access, adherence, and safe transitions.
answer_b: Exclude them because social concerns are not related to nursing case management.
answer_c: Document them only if the payer asks for additional information.
answer_d: Treat them as patient preferences that do not require follow-up.
rationale: Transportation and food access can determine whether ordered care and teaching are usable, so they belong in patient-centered intake and coordination planning.

question_text: Which documentation best reflects health literacy assessment during intake?
answer_a: Patient was educated and appeared to understand.
answer_b: Patient used teach-back to describe two wound infection warning signs and requested large-print supply instructions.
answer_c: Caregiver seems responsible for teaching the patient.
answer_d: Patient has low literacy and may not follow directions.
rationale: Specific teach-back results and requested communication supports provide objective evidence of understanding and needed adaptations.

question_text: Which goal is most measurable and patient-centered for the initial M02 care plan?
answer_a: Patient will improve diabetes management soon.
answer_b: Patient will be compliant with all instructions.
answer_c: Within seven days, patient will describe the current insulin schedule using teach-back and identify who to contact for out-of-range readings.
answer_d: RN will complete all case management tasks as available.
rationale: The goal includes a time frame, patient action, teach-back evidence, and a safety follow-up element tied to the patient's medication-risk finding.

question_text: Which intake finding should generally rank highest when building the initial priority list?
answer_a: A routine request to update the preferred pharmacy later this month.
answer_b: A preference for morning phone calls when scheduling allows.
answer_c: A completed demographic field that needs formatting cleanup.
answer_d: A current medication discrepancy paired with patient confusion after discharge.
rationale: A medication discrepancy with confusion creates immediate safety risk and should rank above routine administrative or preference-based findings.

question_text: Which note is the most objective and audit-ready?
answer_a: Patient reported two missed wound visits because transportation was unavailable; RN documented supply status and initiated follow-up per policy.
answer_b: Patient is careless and does not follow the wound plan.
answer_c: Family is unreliable and should be replaced.
answer_d: The home situation is bad and probably unsafe.
rationale: Objective documentation states what was reported, the relevant barrier, and the RN action without unsupported labels or blame.

question_text: The referral and patient report conflict about weight-bearing limits after discharge. What should the RN case manager do?
answer_a: Choose the limit that seems most practical for the patient.
answer_b: Document the conflict and coordinate timely clarification with the provider or authorized team contact before relying on the plan.
answer_c: Ignore the conflict because it is not a medication issue.
answer_d: Tell the patient there are no restrictions until another visit occurs.
rationale: Conflicting orders or instructions can create safety risk; the RN should document the discrepancy and seek clarification without making a provider-only decision.

question_text: How should caregiver input be handled when it differs from what the patient reports?
answer_a: Use the caregiver report only because caregivers are usually more accurate.
answer_b: Use the patient report only and delete caregiver concerns.
answer_c: Document each source, identify the conflict, assess risk, and plan follow-up to resolve the gap.
answer_d: Treat the conflict as proof that the case should be closed.
rationale: Caregiver input is a source of assessment data; conflicts should be made visible and followed up rather than ignored or treated as proof of fault.

question_text: A referral lacks wound supply delivery status and the patient is unsure whether supplies are coming. What is the best documentation practice?
answer_a: Leave the issue out until all supply questions are fully resolved.
answer_b: Write that supplies are probably available because they were ordered.
answer_c: Mark the plan complete and ask the next nurse to check later.
answer_d: Record the missing information, assign follow-up ownership, set a time frame, and update the plan when status is confirmed.
rationale: Audit-ready care planning can include unresolved gaps when the record identifies the gap, owner, due time, and plan for verification.

question_text: Which care plan element most improves audit readiness after structured intake?
answer_a: A link between each priority finding, RN action, responsible contact, time frame, and follow-up evidence.
answer_b: A long narrative with no dates, owners, or next steps.
answer_c: A statement that the patient has complex needs without details.
answer_d: A note that all issues will be handled later if time allows.
rationale: Audit-ready plans let a reviewer trace assessment findings to actions, responsibilities, timing, and evidence of follow-up.

question_text: Which statement best reflects the certificate release rule for this draft module?
answer_a: A certificate can be released when the learner views the overview page.
answer_b: No certificate releases until provider approval metadata and required completion evidence gates are satisfied.
answer_c: The M02 quiz alone authorizes certificate release.
answer_d: A certificate can be released if the learner writes a strong reflection.
rationale: Certificate release is blocked while approval metadata is unavailable and until all required completion, assessment, and attestation evidence gates are met.

question_text: In the intake priority builder, a learner ranks medication confusion, near fall, missing supplies, transportation barrier, and food insecurity as the top five. What is the best action map?
answer_a: Map all five findings to routine chart review only.
answer_b: Map all five findings to payer authorization only.
answer_c: Connect each finding to a specific RN case management action, communication partner, time frame, and documentation evidence.
answer_d: Remove social and functional findings so the action map contains only diagnoses.
rationale: The interaction requires prioritization plus action mapping; each high-priority finding should lead to a specific coordination, teaching, escalation, or follow-up step.
